questions.prompt: What's the difference between wall time and CPU time in Workers?

choices.text: Wall time is total request duration including time waiting on subrequests (fetches to APIs, databases, R2). CPU time is only time the JS engine actively spent computing — parsing, looping, crypto. The Workers CPU limit (10ms free, 30s paid) caps CPU time, not wall time, so a Worker can spend several seconds awaiting a slow upstream and stay well under the budget. The thing that blows the limit is local computation: tight loops, large JSON parsing, synchronous crypto. Understanding the distinction tells you whether 'my Worker is slow' is a CPU problem (refactor or offload to Queues) or a wall-time problem (fix the upstream).

questions.explanation: Wall time includes I/O waits; CPU time is only active computation. The limit caps CPU, not wall time.

questions.prompt: Worker that loops over 500 items, fetching detail from an API for each one. Started failing with 'Too many subrequests' under load. Fix?

choices.text: Subrequest limit is 1000 on paid (50 on free) — 500 individual fetches is too brittle. Options: (1) use the API's bulk endpoint if it exists — one fetch for all items, (2) move the work to a Queue handler that processes batches of 10-50 per invocation, each handler stays within budget, (3) cache aggressively so most items hit cache instead of the API. Loops of N fetches are an anti-pattern in Workers — they create coupling between traffic volume and per-request fan-out. Batching is the structural fix; raising the plan only delays the failure.

questions.explanation: Switch to bulk endpoints, queue the work, or cache aggressively. N-fetches-per-request is structurally broken.
